value.Title: EVCS Surpasses 100 Million Electric Miles Powered by Its Charging Network

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJP-44C3-S4RP-X1KV-00000-00EVCS Surpasses 100 Million Electric Miles Powered by Its Charging Network2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:09:37ZLexisNexisEVCS Surpasses 100 Million Electric Miles Powered by Its Charging Network

100 million miles driven reflects growing EV adoption and the critical role of public fast-charging

LOS ANGELES, CA / ACCESS Newswire / May 12, 2026 / EVCS, one of the largest electric vehicle (EV) fast-charging networks on the West Coast, announced today that drivers have collectively powered more than 100 million electric miles on its network. The achievement underscores the company's mission to make fast, affordable, and renewable-powered charging accessible to all.

With more than 300 locations, EVCS has built a robust footprint across California, Oregon, and Washington. Every kilowatt on the EVCS network is backed by 100% renewable energy, making it easier for drivers and communities to accelerate the transition to clean transportation. EVCS also offers flexible subscription plans designed to reduce charging costs for frequent drivers, including high-mileage and gig economy users.

"This milestone is more than a number. It shows that reliable and accessible public charging is accelerating the shift to electric," said Eric Danner, CEO of EVCS. "By combining a growing fast-charging network with flexible subscription options, we're empowering drivers to go electric with confidence and affordability."

The 100 million miles driven on the EVCS network represent a significant reduction in CO₂ emissions compared to gasoline-powered driving, contributing to cleaner air and a healthier planet for the communities EVCS serves.

EVCS is continuing to expand its network, with new locations and upgraded equipment coming online every month, building the infrastructure needed for the next 100 million miles and beyond.

About EVCS:

EVCS was founded in 2018 and has quickly become the largest privately held, electric vehicle fast charging network on the West Coast, home to 50% of the EVs in the U.S. EVCS is committed to leading the electric transportation revolution by rapidly expanding access to fast, affordable, reliable, and conveniently located public EV charging. Powered by 100% renewable energy, EVCS is disrupting the mobility industry through an approach that utilizes public and private funding sources to accelerate the installation of fast charging stations. EVCS has secured private and public funding to install, own, and operate over 1,500 chargers across over 300 public and private site hosts, including Fortune 500 companies and underrepresented communities. In addition, EVCS offers flexible subscription charging plans to fit a variety of driving needs. These include options tailored for gig and high-mileage drivers, providing significant potential savings. For more information, visit www.evcs.com.

MEDIA CONTACTS:

Justin Arita
Senior Growth Manager, EVCS
justina@evcs.com

value.Title: The race to unlock ‘superhot’ geothermal energy is heating up. Here’s why it matters

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJF-B9C3-S45D-418J-00000-00The race to unlock ‘superhot’ geothermal energy is heating up. Here’s why it matters2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:09:37ZLexisNexisThe race to unlock ‘superhot’ geothermal energy is heating up. Here’s why it matters

Harnessing the power of just one per cent of superhot rocks could provide more than eight times the current global electricity generation.

Deep beneath the Earth’s surface lies an immense source of energy that scientists believe could help power a low-carbon future.

A growing number of researchers and energy companies are now racing to unlock ‘superhot’ geothermal energy, a very old – but also novel – form of power that could provide constant, carbon-free electricity almost anywhere on Earth.

Earlier this year, the International Energy Agency (IEA) highlighted superhot geothermal in its State of Energy Innovation report, describing it as a promising source of “clean, firm power” capable of supporting the transition away from fossil fuels.

Now, one of the sector’s most closely watched projects is breaking ground in the US state of Oregon, where startup Quaise Energy says it plans to build what it calls the world’s first superhot geothermal power plant by 2030.

What is superhot geothermal energy?

Geothermal energy uses heat from beneath the Earth’s surface to generate electricity or provide heating.

Its role as a power or heating source isn’t necessarily new.

In Iceland, geothermal water has been used to heat homes for nearly a century. Today, around 30 per cent of the country’s electricity comes from geothermal sources.

According to the IEA, traditional geothermal plants rely on naturally occurring underground reservoirs of hot water or steam, which are typically concentrated in volcanically active regions or along the boundaries of the Earth’s tectonic plate.

Related

Geothermal energy could replace 42% of EU's fossil electricity. Which nation has the most potential?

'Turn off the taps before mopping the floor': Renewables more cost effective than direct air capture

Superhot geothermal aims to go deeper.

The technology targets rocks that are hotter than 300°C, where water reaches a supercritical state and can carry significantly more energy than conventional geothermal systems.

According to the US-based non-profit Clean Air Task Force, tapping just one per cent of these resources could provide more than eight times the current electricity generation globally.

Why is it difficult?

The main challenge, until now, has been drilling deep enough to reach these extreme temperatures.

According to various energy agencies, conventional drilling systems – many adapted from the oil and gas industry – face major challenges under the extreme heat and pressure found several kilometres underground. Costs also rise as wells get deeper.

That has led researchers to explore alternative drilling technologies.

Quaise Energy plans to use conventional drilling for the upper sections of its Oregon wells before switching to millimetre wave technology developed at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, where the startup was spun out of research projects.

The system uses high-frequency electromagnetic waves, similar to microwaves, to melt and vaporise rock rather than cutting through it mechanically.

If successful, the process could allow wells to reach much deeper geothermal resources than existing technology permits.

Water would then be pumped underground, heated by the surrounding rock and returned to the surface as steam to generate electricity before being recycled back into the system.

Quaise says this system would offer 50 megawatts of always-on, renewable power – enough to supply tens of thousands of homes. The company hopes to expand the project to 200 megawatts soon after coming online, a potential game-changer for a world struggling to cut emissions while keeping up with rising energy demand.

Why does it matter?

Unlike solar and wind power, geothermal energy can run continuously regardless of weather conditions. However, the plummeting cost of battery storage is helping renewables provide around-the-clock electricity at prices that rival fossil fuels, according to a new report from the International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA).

Supporters also point to geothermal’s relatively small land footprint compared with large solar or wind farms.

It’s little surprise that interest in superhot geothermal is growing globally.

In Iceland, researchers recently secured €10 million in EU funding to develop similar projects. Last year, New Zealand also entered a cooperative agreement with Iceland to develop geothermal technology as part of its long-term energy security plans.

Related

Inside the underwater turbine farm set to generate clean energy from France’s tides

Clean energy from rain? Scientists generate electricity from falling droplets

Experts believe this type of power could eventually expand beyond volcanically active regions. According to the IEA, advances in deep drilling could make it viable across larger parts of Europe, Asia and North America.

Promising as it may be, the technology has a long way to go until it transforms power grids globally.

No commercial superhot geothermal plant is operating yet, and researchers still need to prove that drilling systems, underground rock formations and power infrastructure can withstand the extreme conditions over long periods.

There may be environmental concerns, too.

Researchers say geothermal drilling can trigger small earthquakes – a phenomenon called induced seismicity. While most are too small to be felt, some can be serious.

In 2017, an earthquake measuring 5.4 on the Richter scale struck near a geothermal site in Pohang, South Korea, causing widespread damage. It was believed to be caused by induced seismicity, after high-pressure fluid was injected into the ground at the site.

Still, its advocates argue, the potential returns are difficult to ignore.

According to the Clean Air Task Force, about two per cent of the geothermal energy located between three and 10 kilometres beneath the Earth’s surface could provide the equivalent of 2,000 times the current energy demand of the United States alone.

value.Title: Wave energy firm Panthalassa nets USD 140m from Peter Thiel, others

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHT-X6S3-S9B9-13MM-00000-00Wave energy firm Panthalassa nets USD 140m from Peter Thiel, others2026-05-08T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:09:37ZLexisNexisWave energy firm Panthalassa nets USD 140m from Peter Thiel, othersSeeNews

May 8 (Renewables Now) - US renewable energy and ocean technology company Panthalassa has closed a USD-140-million (USD 119.1m) funding round, led by entrepreneur and venture capitalist Peter Thiel, to scale its technology for wave-powered offshore AI computing systems with a pilot manufacturing site, among other initiatives.

Panthalassa is developing autonomous floating units that generate electricity from ocean waves and use the power on-board to run AI computing systems at sea. The company says the surrounding seawater helps cool the equipment, while processed data is transmitted to land by satellites instead of sending electricity back to shore. Its Ocean-1, Ocean-2 and Wavehopper prototypes underwent successful testing in 2021 and 2024, while this year it plans to deploy its Ocean-3 pilot node series in the northern Pacific Ocean.

The new capital received will be used to complete Panthalassa's pilot manufacturing facility near Portland, Oregon and advance the deployment of its Ocean-3 series of self-propelled nodes.

The Series B funding round was backed by new investors John Doerr, Marc Benioff's TIME Ventures, Max Levchin's SciFi Ventures, Susquehanna Sustainable Investments, Hanwha Asset Management (USA)'s venture fund, Anthony Pratt, Fortescue Ventures, Future Positive, WTI, Nimble Partners, Super Micro Computer, Sozo Ventures, Dylan Field, Planetary VC, Leblon Capital, Resilience Reserve, Portland Seed Fund and the Intrepid Oregon Fund. Returning investors also participated, including Founders Fund, Gigascale Capital, Lowercarbon Capital, Unless and WovenEarth.

(USD 1 = EUR 0.850)

value.Title: They're building data centers in the ocean

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJ7-CWB3-SC7F-R4TV-00000-00They're building data centers in the ocean2026-05-09T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:09:37ZLexisNexisThey're building data centers in the ocean

In the face of protests over the construction of large AI data centers in U.S. cities and doubts about whether such projects can be realized on land, the startup Panthalassa is opting for an offshore strategy. To that end, the renewable energy and ocean technology company announced a $140 million funding round led by Peter Thiel, a venture capitalist and co-founder of PayPal and Palantir Technologies. The funding will enable the company to complete a pilot manufacturing plant near Portland, Oregon, and accelerate the deployment of its Ocean-3 node series, which will perform AI inference calculations at sea using energy generated by ocean waves.

"There are three energy sources on the planet with a potential capacity of tens of terawatts: solar, nuclear, and the open ocean," said Garth Sheldon-Coulson, CEO of Panthalassa. "We have developed a technology platform that operates in the regions with the highest energy-density waves on the planet, far from the coast, and transforms that resource into clean, reliable energy. We are now ready to build factories, deploy fleets, and provide a new source of sustainable energy for humanity," he added. Panthalassa's nodes are autonomous floating energy systems, mass-produced from steel plates in coastal factories. They operate on the high seas, where they generate clean electricity 24 hours a day, and instead of transmitting the energy to onshore grids, Panthalassa uses it directly on board to power AI chips, sending inference tokens ashore via satellite. The surrounding ocean provides superior cooling at no cost, solving one of the biggest engineering challenges in land-based data centers and extending the chips' lifespan. As demand for electricity and computing power continues to rise, land-based data centers face growing constraints: limited grid capacity, water shortages for cooling, supply chain bottlenecks, permit delays, and impacts on local communities and infrastructure.

value.Title: 70% of Portland -area adults say they're directly affected by Trump policies. Here's how An increase in the cost-of-living topped the list of negative effects poll respondents cited. Other top complaints included fear-inducing immigration enforcement, tariffs and hits to the job market.
value.Overview: The Oregonian: Web Edition (Portland, Oregon), POLITICS, 1915words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK5-4833-SDF3-C4G0-00000-0070% of Portland-area adults say they're directly affected by Trump policies. Here's how 2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:09:37ZLexisNexisPOLITICS70% of Portland-area adults say they're directly affected by Trump policies. Here's how An increase in the cost-of-living topped the list of negative effects poll respondents cited. Other top complaints included fear-inducing immigration enforcement, tariffs and hits to the job market.Betsy Hammond

More than 70% of Portland-area adults say they have been directly affected by Trump administration policies or executive orders and three-fourths of those say the impact has been negative, according to a new poll commissioned by The Oregonian/OregonLive.

An increase in the cost of living topped the list of negative impacts poll respondents cited. Other top complaints included fear-inducing immigration enforcement, tariffs and hits to the job market.

A narrower slice of Portland-area residents, nearly 1 in 5, said President Donald Trump's policies have directly affected them for the better. Most are Republicans who said he lowered their taxes or cracked down on illegal immigration or both.

window.addEventListener("message",function(a){if(void 0!==a.data["datawrapper-height"]){var e=document.querySelectorAll("iframe");for(var t in a.data["datawrapper-height"])for(var r,i=0;r=e[i];i++)if(r.contentWindow===a.source){var d=a.data["datawrapper-height"][t]+"px";r.style.height=d}}});

People who live in the Democrat-dominated city of Portland were particularly likely to say the president's actions have harmed them personally, with three-fourths saying they've experienced a direct impact and more than 90% of those saying that's been negative.

Rae Blackbird, a Portland native who graduated from University of California, Berkeley last May with a degree in public policy, long dreamed of working on the environment, and a close college friend graduated with a job already lined up at the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency.

But after returning to Portland and applying for "basically everything I could find," primarily in the green energy sector, Blackbird watched his friend's job at the EPA evaporate due to DOGE cuts then found himself shut out of green jobs and working instead as a legal assistant at a public defense firm.

Trump policies have forced Blackbird to "table quite a bit of my environmental ambitions," he said. "The world that we studied to graduate into is not that world that we entered the workforce in. There's that personal impact on people's lives and also the large societal loss that people were energized and excited and ready to work to make a difference, but there's just no opportunities."

The poll, which covered a wide range of topics, was conducted by Portland-based DHM Research from April 23 to April 30. It reached 600 registered voters, and the results were weighted to accurately reflect the populations of Multnomah, Washington and Clackamas counties. Its overall margin of error is plus or minus 4 percentage points for the entire metro area, with larger error bands for subpopulations, such as the city of Portland or unaffiliated voters.

The ways in which residents perceive themselves to have been impacted by the Trump administration were captured via one of just two open-ended questions in the newsroom's poll. Responses were often quite detailed.

"As a member of the LGBTQ+ community, I worry that my rights are being eroded," respondent Greg Nika of Portland told a poll taker. "I'm concerned about attacks on voting by mail. I'm concerned about more restrictive vaccine access. I'm concerned about rolling back investment in clean energy. I'm concerned about funding being revoked, including for the Interstate Bridge."

"ICE took away a friend of ours and also our landscaper; tariffs have impacted my business," another respondent, who did not grant permission to use his name, wrote.

"I worked in public health research and grant administration, and the illegal funding cuts and policy actions have destroyed the future of that field," wrote another critic of Trump's actions. "I now work in another field entirely after 15-plus years in public health.

window.addEventListener("message",function(a){if(void 0!==a.data["datawrapper-height"]){var e=document.querySelectorAll("iframe");for(var t in a.data["datawrapper-height"])for(var r,i=0;r=e[i];i++)if(r.contentWindow===a.source){var d=a.data["datawrapper-height"][t]+"px";r.style.height=d}}});

Some fans of Trump policies were just as specific.

"My paychecks are better," said poll respondent Sean Simpson, who lives in Washington County. Simpson cited "tax credits, secure border and better peace of mind" as other positives he's experienced due to Trump policies.

Another poll respondent, who did not grant permission to use their name, told a poll taker, "Our investments are up, we did better on our taxes than previously and I'll be able to afford a weight loss medication in July thanks to the Bridge program," referencing the Trump administration-initiated GLP-1 Bridge Program that will allow some Medicare Part D patients to get weight loss medications for just $50 a month.

window.addEventListener("message",function(a){if(void 0!==a.data["datawrapper-height"]){var e=document.querySelectorAll("iframe");for(var t in a.data["datawrapper-height"])for(var r,i=0;r=e[i];i++)if(r.contentWindow===a.source){var d=a.data["datawrapper-height"][t]+"px";r.style.height=d}}});

Latinos most likely to cite harms, high income people to cite tax breaks

No demographic group skewed more negative on Trump's effects on them than Portland-area Latinos. Ten percent of Latino voters said Trump's orders and policies have had a positive impact on them, while 65% reported negative effects.

High-income Oregonians, in households earning more than $100,000 and more than $150,000, were among those most likely to report that Trump's policies had helped them, likely reflecting the tax breaks he championed for many high-income individuals and most seniors.

Unsurprisingly, opinion splits by party were stark. Across the metro area, 75% of registered Democrats said Trump's policies and orders had personally harmed them, whereas only 11% of Republicans said so. On the flip side, 48% of Republicans said Trump had directly helped them versus only 8% of Democrats.

Republicans were also far more likely than Democrats or unaffiliated voters to say they have not been directly affected by a Trump order or policy, at 35%.

More than half of poll respondents who said Trump had a positive personal impact on them cited one of two factors: taxes and immigration. Overall, poll respondents who cited a Trump administration impact on their taxes were overwhelmingly positive about that.

Among the much larger group of Portland-area adults who said Trump had personally affected them in a negative way, views of how were more dispersed: About 40% cited an increase in the cost of living, but notable numbers also cited employment struggles (17%), immigration enforcement (16%), tariffs (16%) and cutbacks to green jobs, renewable energy and electric car rebates.

The position that Trump's primary personal impact on poll respondents was on the cost of living held relatively constant across the three metro counties but was particularly pronounced in Portland City Council District 1, east of Interstate 205. That area has the highest poverty rate of the city's four council districts.

A one-two punch

For Aubrey Williams, a professional artist who lives in that district, March brought a particularly painful economic one-two punch, she said. Trump's launch of the war in Iran quickly drove up gas prices and some related costs, then Williams lost her dream job as a concept artist for video games and with it, her healthcare insurance. She blames the elimination of her job on what she says has been the Trump administration's weak regulation of AI.

She's struggling to afford food as well as medications her life depends on while receiving unemployment benefits. She's afraid to seek an adjustment to her income-dependent student loan payments because Trump has changed the rules on student loans so often, she said.

"It's so intersectional, all of the reasons why everything is going wrong and having it all lead back to him," Williams said.

For Washington County residents, Trump's orders and policies on immigration raids and ICE crackdowns figured prominently -- and largely but not entirely negatively -- in people's lives. Twenty-three percent of the county's adults reported immigration enforcement and ICE as a direct effect on their lives.

Tax cut makes homeownership more affordable

Across the metro area, the second-most-commonly cited impact was on reducing taxes. But that impact barely registered for Multnomah County adults, with only 8% citing it as a primary impact, whereas Washington and Clackamas residents cited taxes a little more than twice that often. Republicans across the three-county metro area were highly likely to say Trump impacted their tax burden, with 48% of them naming that as the main effect on them personally. Just 4% of Democrats cited an impact on their taxes.

For Kristopher Hess, who lives in the Aloha area and works 12-hour swing shifts at a factory that operates 24-7, the end of taxes on overtime has had a big effect. He and his co-workers are automatically scheduled to work eight hours of overtime every other week with the extra pay bump now tax-free.

His and his wife's tax refund was noticeably larger this year than in the past, and his paychecks are bigger this year due to less tax withholding, he said. That's a particularly welcome development since the couple, in their early 30s, just managed to buy their first home. "It's been very helpful," said Hess, who is a registered Republican but said he doesn't consider himself a party die-hard.

For working-age adults, Trump's impact on employment and the job market was top of mind for many, ranging from 13% to 19% across different age ranges, with those ages 30 to 44 most likely to cite it as a direct impact. And only one of the 600 people who answered the poll said Trump's impact on the job market helped rather than harmed them.

But adults aged 65 and up showed almost no personal impact from Trump's impact on employment, with just 3% listing it.

On the flip side, senior citizens were the only age group in which a significant slice, 14%, said Trump had personally affected them by changing the political climate. They differed, however, on whether that was a positive or a negative, with roughly half going each way.

A hit to mental health

A significant chunk of city of Portland residents, 13%, said Trump policies and orders personally impacted their emotional well-being. Very few residents outside Portland, just 4%, mentioned that as a personal impact.

Rachel Silvis of Portland said Trump's actions have left her feeling hopeless much of the time.

"I'm scared for the future and I'm scared for right now," she said. "I just don't like waking up every single day and there's something on the news that doesn't make any sense," including the war in Iran, lessening protections on national parks and empowering ICE agents to tear families apart, she said.

Silvis recently completed training to work as an esthetician. But she said she, like 90% of the people she trained with, hasn't been able to find a job in the field, a problem she attributes to slumping employment and economic optimism under Trump. So she's looking for other work.

"The economy is crashing and it's hard for people to get jobs," Silvis said. "And then it's like, is the government being honest with us? Nobody knows. It's destabilizing to your feeling that you can see a better future. Are we going to have fresh water and clean air in 10 years with all this stuff going on? It's very scary. It's something that I try not to think about too often because it genuinely freaks me out."

value.Title: Portland General Electric 10-Q: Capital Commitment to Hybrid Renewable Assets Driving Rate Base and 11 Other Management Decisions

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJX-8203-RTF0-P04G-00000-00Portland General Electric 10-Q: Capital Commitment to Hybrid Renewable Assets Driving Rate Base and 11 Other Management Decisions2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:09:37ZLexisNexisPortland General Electric 10-Q: Capital Commitment to Hybrid Renewable Assets Driving Rate Base and 11 Other Management Decisions

NYSE STOCK REPORT

Management finalized agreements under the 2023 All-Source RFP for two solar and battery hybrid projects totaling 615 MW. The company expects to invest roughly $1.03 billion in these assets, excluding AFUDC. The projects include full ownership of the 250 MW Biglow Optimization and partial ownership of the 365 MW Wheatridge Expansion. Both target commercial operation by the end of 2027. You benefit from concrete renewable capacity additions that secure expiring tax credits, advance decarbonization, and expand the rate base. These projects stem from a competitive procurement process designed to maximize customer value and system reliability.

Strategic Washington Expansion to Drive Revenue Diversification

On February 15, 2026, management agreed to buy specific Washington state utility operations from PacifiCorp for $1.9 billion. This move adds roughly 140,000 customers, 805 MW of generation, and 4,500 miles of lines across 2,700 square miles. The company will manage these assets as a separate subsidiary under Washington regulation. You benefit from increased scale and long-term growth without passing direct costs to existing Oregon or Washington customers. Management expects to close the deal in about twelve months, pending state approvals. While this introduces timing risk, committed financing and a joint-venture partner back the execution. This transaction aims to boost earnings per share and free cash flow while keeping majority ownership and operational control.

Joint Venture Formation to Optimize Balance Sheet Flexibility

Management formed a joint venture with Manulife Infrastructure Fund III, L.P. to hold a minority stake in the new Washington utility business. Manulife will provide up to $600 million in equity toward the $1.9 billion purchase. PGE retains majority ownership and sole operator status. This funding structure lowers our equity burden and maintains financial flexibility. It aligns with our strategy to fund the deal through a balanced mix of debt, equity, and partner capital. You gain a lower-risk expansion with less capital intensity, while the company keeps strategic control. The joint venture allows us to execute the acquisition without altering current customer rates.

Elevated Capital Expenditure Plan Advancing Grid Modernization

The 2026 capital expenditure plan reaches approximately $1.655 billion, excluding AFUDC. This spending targets grid modernization, renewable energy, and customer growth. Management expects capital deployment to stay elevated at $1.3 to $1.7 billion annually through 2030. You see direct value through rate-base growth that supports future earnings while meeting rising load demands and decarbonization targets. We plan to fund these investments using $1.0 to $1.2 billion in operating cash flows, alongside long-term debt and equity issuances. Management continues a disciplined capital deployment strategy that balances heavy investment needs with customer affordability.

Proactive Equity Financing to Support Capital Deployment

Management secured equity forward sale and at-the-market offering agreements to fund growth. A February 2026 agreement for 10.8 million shares could yield $530 million, building on $300 million raised through earlier programs. The company will use these proceeds for general corporate purposes, non-emitting capacity, and the Washington acquisition. While this causes modest dilution, you gain non-dilutive capital at settlement that protects credit metrics and limits excessive debt. Remaining forward positions give us the flexibility to time capital raises with actual spending. This approach highlights a prudent equity financing strategy during a cycle of elevated capital deployment.

Robust Debt Financing Strategy Preserving Liquidity

The company secured new debt funding, including a $350 million, 24-month term loan, drawing $170 million by mid-April 2026. Management also amended the acquisition bridge facility, reducing it to $1.3 billion. Along with revolving credit and commercial paper, our available liquidity stands strong at $954 million as of March 31, 2026. You benefit from this cost-effective, diversified funding that covers capital spending and the upcoming acquisition while protecting investment-grade credit ratings. Debt covenants remain secure, with debt-to-total capital at 53.1%. This strategic debt management keeps interest expenses manageable as we execute our capital plan.

Corporate Reorganization Initiated for Enhanced Financial Agility

In July 2025, management applied to the OPUC to create a holding company structure, placing a non-operating corporate entity above the existing utility. This shift provides financial agility for reliability planning, economic development, and the pending Washington acquisition. Shares will convert one-for-one, causing no change in relative ownership. You gain improved access to capital markets and a structurally efficient entity built for growth. The application is in the evidentiary phase, with an OPUC order expected in August 2026. This foundational corporate restructure holds significant long-term implications for our balance sheet and growth initiatives.

Resource Procurement Advancing Clean Energy Capacity Targets

Management sent the final 2025 All-Source RFP shortlist to the OPUC and began commercial negotiations. We are prioritizing renewable and non-emitting dispatchable capacity, targeting about 2,500 MW. The shortlist features battery, wind, solar, and hybrid projects using both power purchase and build-transfer agreements. This addresses 2029 capacity needs while advancing state decarbonization goals. You see accelerated progress toward clean energy through cost-effective resources that keep power affordable and reliable. We favor projects launching between 2028 and 2030, which can drive future earnings through owned assets. Management expects OPUC acknowledgment by May 2026.

Strategic Transmission Investment Expanding Regional Market Access

Management signed a non-binding memorandum of understanding to take a 20% stake in the North Plains Connector. This 415-mile high-voltage transmission project represents a $3.2 billion investment, offset by a $700 million federal grant. The line connects three regional markets, relieving congestion and addressing load growth. You gain from expanded transmission capacity that opens wholesale market access and boosts reliability. Federal funding helps lower the net cost of this infrastructure. We continue to negotiate with the Department of Energy, and final terms will dictate our material capital commitment. This investment strictly aligns with our integrated resource planning needs.

Long-Term Resource Strategy Reaffirming Decarbonization Targets

Management submitted an updated Clean Energy Plan and Integrated Resource Plan to the OPUC on June 18, 2025. The filing outlines a new Preferred Portfolio but leaves the previously acknowledged 2023 Action Plan intact. We refined our capacity needs to require 3,500 to 4,500 MW of renewable and non-emitting resources. You receive a clear view of our 20-year resource strategy, designed to hit 80% emission reductions by 2030 and 100% by 2040. This approach sustains our clean energy momentum without triggering immediate rate-case impacts. The plan underscores management's commitment to an equitable, affordable, and reliable energy transition.

Proactive Wildfire Mitigation Investments Protecting Asset Resiliency

The company filed its 2026-2028 Wildfire Mitigation Plan with the OPUC. Management forecasts $47 to $50 million in annual operating costs and $70 to $84 million in capital investments for system hardening and vegetation management. We already invested $14 million in related projects during the first quarter of 2026. You benefit from proactive risk mitigation that limits wildfire liability and reduces power shutoff events. We expect to recover these costs through rates, subject to prudency reviews. This plan proves management's dedication to public safety and financial defense in a high-risk environment.

Disciplined Regulatory Strategy Defending Cost Recovery and Margins

Management continues pushing to recover deferred costs through established regulatory mechanisms. We are actively contesting specific disallowances and earnings tests from recent OPUC orders. First-quarter results showed $15 million in charges from storm and cost disallowances. However, management believes we will recover the remaining $44 million in storm and $80 million in associated deferred balances. You should watch rate-case outcomes closely, as they directly impact earned returns and cash flows. We remain heavily engaged in regulatory dockets involving large loads and data centers to protect customer affordability and our bottom line. This disciplined approach smooths earnings volatility while defending essential capital investments.

This transaction aims to boost earnings per share and free cash flow while keeping majority ownership and operational control.

Joint Venture Formation to Optimize Balance Sheet Flexibility

Management formed a joint venture with Manulife Infrastructure Fund III, L.P. to hold a minority stake in the new Washington utility business. Manulife will provide up to $600 million in equity toward the $1.9 billion purchase. PGE retains majority ownership and sole operator status. This funding structure lowers our equity burden and maintains financial flexibility. It aligns with our strategy to fund the deal through a balanced mix of debt, equity, and partner capital. You gain a lower-risk expansion with less capital intensity, while the company keeps strategic control. The joint venture allows us to execute the acquisition without altering current customer rates.

Elevated Capital Expenditure Plan Advancing Grid Modernization

The 2026 capital expenditure plan reaches approximately $1.655 billion, excluding AFUDC. This spending targets grid modernization, renewable energy, and customer growth. Management expects capital deployment to stay elevated at $1.3 to $1.7 billion annually through 2030. You see direct value through rate-base growth that supports future earnings while meeting rising load demands and decarbonization targets. We plan to fund these investments using $1.0 to $1.2 billion in operating cash flows, alongside long-term debt and equity issuances. Management continues a disciplined capital deployment strategy that balances heavy investment needs with customer affordability.

Proactive Equity Financing to Support Capital Deployment

Management secured equity forward sale and at-the-market offering agreements to fund growth. A February 2026 agreement for 10.8 million shares could yield $530 million, building on $300 million raised through earlier programs. The company will use these proceeds for general corporate purposes, non-emitting capacity, and the Washington acquisition. While this causes modest dilution, you gain non-dilutive capital at settlement that protects credit metrics and limits excessive debt. Remaining forward positions give us the flexibility to time capital raises with actual spending. This approach highlights a prudent equity financing strategy during a cycle of elevated capital deployment.

Robust Debt Financing Strategy Preserving Liquidity

The company secured new debt funding, including a $350 million, 24-month term loan, drawing $170 million by mid-April 2026. Management also amended the acquisition bridge facility, reducing it to $1.3 billion. Along with revolving credit and commercial paper, our available liquidity stands strong at $954 million as of March 31, 2026. You benefit from this cost-effective, diversified funding that covers capital spending and the upcoming acquisition while protecting investment-grade credit ratings. Debt covenants remain secure, with debt-to-total capital at 53.1%. This strategic debt management keeps interest expenses manageable as we execute our capital plan.

Corporate Reorganization Initiated for Enhanced Financial Agility

In July 2025, management applied to the OPUC to create a holding company structure, placing a non-operating corporate entity above the existing utility. This shift provides financial agility for reliability planning, economic development, and the pending Washington acquisition. Shares will convert one-for-one, causing no change in relative ownership. You gain improved access to capital markets and a structurally efficient entity built for growth. The application is in the evidentiary phase, with an OPUC order expected in August 2026. This foundational corporate restructure holds significant long-term implications for our balance sheet and growth initiatives.

Capital Commitment to Hybrid Renewable Assets Driving Rate Base

Management finalized agreements under the 2023 All-Source RFP for two solar and battery hybrid projects totaling 615 MW. The company expects to invest roughly $1.03 billion in these assets, excluding AFUDC. The projects include full ownership of the 250 MW Biglow Optimization and partial ownership of the 365 MW Wheatridge Expansion. Both target commercial operation by the end of 2027. You benefit from concrete renewable capacity additions that secure expiring tax credits, advance decarbonization, and expand the rate base. These projects stem from a competitive procurement process designed to maximize customer value and system reliability.

Strategic Transmission Investment Expanding Regional Market Access

Management signed a non-binding memorandum of understanding to take a 20% stake in the North Plains Connector. This 415-mile high-voltage transmission project represents a $3.2 billion investment, offset by a $700 million federal grant. The line connects three regional markets, relieving congestion and addressing load growth. You gain from expanded transmission capacity that opens wholesale market access and boosts reliability. Federal funding helps lower the net cost of this infrastructure. We continue to negotiate with the Department of Energy, and final terms will dictate our material capital commitment. This investment strictly aligns with our integrated resource planning needs.

Long-Term Resource Strategy Reaffirming Decarbonization Targets

Management submitted an updated Clean Energy Plan and Integrated Resource Plan to the OPUC on June 18, 2025. The filing outlines a new Preferred Portfolio but leaves the previously acknowledged 2023 Action Plan intact. We refined our capacity needs to require 3,500 to 4,500 MW of renewable and non-emitting resources. You receive a clear view of our 20-year resource strategy, designed to hit 80% emission reductions by 2030 and 100% by 2040. This approach sustains our clean energy momentum without triggering immediate rate-case impacts. The plan underscores management's commitment to an equitable, affordable, and reliable energy transition.

Proactive Wildfire Mitigation Investments Protecting Asset Resiliency

The company filed its 2026-2028 Wildfire Mitigation Plan with the OPUC. Management forecasts $47 to $50 million in annual operating costs and $70 to $84 million in capital investments for system hardening and vegetation management. We already invested $14 million in related projects during the first quarter of 2026. You benefit from proactive risk mitigation that limits wildfire liability and reduces power shutoff events. We expect to recover these costs through rates, subject to prudency reviews. This plan proves management's dedication to public safety and financial defense in a high-risk environment.

Disciplined Regulatory Strategy Defending Cost Recovery and Margins

Management continues pushing to recover deferred costs through established regulatory mechanisms. We are actively contesting specific disallowances and earnings tests from recent OPUC orders. First-quarter results showed $15 million in charges from storm and cost disallowances. However, management believes we will recover the remaining $44 million in storm and $80 million in associated deferred balances. You should watch rate-case outcomes closely, as they directly impact earned returns and cash flows. We remain heavily engaged in regulatory dockets involving large loads and data centers to protect customer affordability and our bottom line. This disciplined approach smooths earnings volatility while defending essential capital investments.

value.Title: Biogas plant to produce fertilizer

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHM-S4M3-RW69-V1MN-00000-00Biogas plant to produce fertilizer2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:09:37ZLexisNexisTDN.COMBiogas plant to produce fertilizerNICK MORGAN nmorgan@tdn.com

LONGVIEW

Divert Inc. leaders say the fertilizer product it is preparing to produce at its new Longview facility will be welltimed, given disruptions in the global market.

The bioenergy company debuted its facility at the Mint Farm industrial park on April 29, featuring first-of-its-kind technologies that transform inedible food waste into natural gas.

What's produced is then piped directly into the Cascade Natural Gas system.

While the Strait of Hormuz blockade is affecting energy markets, Chris Thomas, Divert's vice president of public affairs, said the fertilizer interference may impact the company more.

"There's a big disruption there," Thomas said.

Energy markets have been getting most of the attention since the beginning of March, when Iran closed the Strait of Hormuz in response to U.S. and Israeli military attacks that began Feb. 28.

But the global fertilizer market has seen similar impacts.

The Persian Gulf is a production hub for nitrogen and phosphate two of the main ingredients in fertilizer, according to Washington, D.C.-based International Food Policy Research Institute.

In 2024, up to 30% of global fertilizer trade passed through the Strait of Hormuz from the Gulf, the institute reports.

With shipments blocked, fertilizer prices are rising.

Plastic-free fertilizer

Thomas said Divert has a nitrogen-rich fertilizer product nearly ready to be sold from the Longview facility.

The company is already selling fertilizer at its Turlock facility, and Longview is starting to produce enough of a nitrogen-rich soil amendment.

Thomas said Divert's technology can separate plastics and other inorganic compounds before the liquid generated from fresh food waste enters a 3 million-gallon anaerobic digester, creating a plastic-free fertilizer option.

Some fertilizers are coated in plastic to control release to ensure plants receive the right amount of nutrients at the right time, but that plastic can seep into soil and water.

Catherine Vecchitto, a process scientist at the Longview plant, said during a facility tour that its soil additive is skimmed from the top of the anaerobic digester liquor.

Scientists regularly test the liquid at different stages for properties such as viscosity, alkalinity and electrical conductivity.

Divert's Longview plant went live in January and began ramping up production in late March after transitioning away from an earlier facility in Albany, Oregon.

Another fertilizer company

Another primary fertilizer nutrient potash could also soon be exported from Longview.

Global agricultural products giant Nutrien is eyeing the Port of Longview for a $500 million to $1 billion plan to export potash fertilizer.

The company aims to make a final decision about whether to build at the Port of Longview's Columbia Riverfront site by 2027 in the newly redeveloped Berth 4.

Nick Morgan is a news reporter for The daily News covering business and the city of Longview.

value.Title: Panthalassa Secures USD140 Million in Series B Financing
value.Overview: MarketLine Financial Deals Tracker, DEAL IN BRIEF, 670words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH7-BR13-S3WP-X2M0-00000-00Panthalassa Secures USD140 Million in Series B Financing2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:09:37ZLexisNexisDEAL IN BRIEFPanthalassa Secures USD140 Million in Series B Financing

Panthalassa, a renewable energy and ocean technology company, announced USD140 million in Series B financing led by Peter Thiel, with participation from new investors John Doerr, Marc Benioff's TIME Ventures, Max Levchin's SciFi Ventures, Susquehanna Sustainable Investments, Hanwha Asset Management (USA)'s venture fund, Anthony Pratt, Fortescue Ventures, Future Positive, WTI, Nimble Partners, Super Micro Computer, Sozo Ventures, Dylan Field, Planetary VC, Leblon Capital, Resilience Reserve, Portland Seed Fund, and the Intrepid Oregon Fund. They are joined by returning investors Founders Fund, Gigascale Capital, Lowercarbon Capital, Unless, and WovenEarth. The funding will complete the company's pilot manufacturing facility near Portland, Oregon, and accelerate deployment of its Ocean-3 series of nodes, which will perform AI inference computing at sea using power generated from ocean waves. "There are three sources of energy on the planet with tens of terawatts of new capacity potential: solar, nuclear, and the open ocean," said Garth Sheldon-Coulson, Co-Founder and CEO of Panthalassa.

"We've built a technology platform that operates in the planet's most energy-dense wave regions, far from shore, and turns that resource into reliable clean power. We're now ready to build factories, deploy fleets, and provide a sustainable new source of energy for humanity." Panthalassa's nodes are autonomous, floating energy systems that are mass-produced from plate steel in coastal factories. They operate in the distant ocean, where they generate clean electricity around the clock. Rather than transmitting energy back to terrestrial grids, Panthalassa uses it directly onboard to power AI chips, sending inference tokens to land by satellite. The surrounding ocean provides free supercooling, addressing one of the largest engineering challenges in land-based data centers and lengthening chip lifetimes. Panthalassa has spent the past decade developing the core power generation, propulsion, autonomy, and at-sea computing technologies behind the platform. Its Ocean-1, Ocean-2, and Wavehopper prototypes in 2021 and 2024 proved these capabilities at sea. In 2026, the company plans to deploy its Ocean-3 pilot node series in the northern Pacific Ocean, demonstrating AI inference capabilities and refining its manufacturing process in preparation for commercial deployments in 2027. As demand for new electricity and computing continues to surge, terrestrial data centers face mounting constraints: limited grid capacity, cooling water scarcity, supply chain bottlenecks, permitting delays, and impacts on local communities and infrastructure. Panthalassa's platform expands energy and AI computing capacity without requiring new data centers or power plants on land, relieving strain on the grid, reducing ecological impacts, and lowering energy costs for families and businesses. "The future demands more compute than we can imagine," said Peter Thiel. "Extra-terrestrial solutions are no longer science fiction. Panthalassa has opened the ocean frontier." "Panthalassa's autonomous wave power system is a game changer in addressing global energy needs and clean power generation," said John Doerr. "It is a triple win: workers benefit, communities benefit, and we gain a strategic asset that strengthens American technological leadership."

April 30th, 2026 ( Ticker Report - Delivered by Newstex )

ESS Tech, Inc. ( NYSE:GWH - Get Free Report) was the target of a large increase in short interest during the month of April. As of April 15th, there was short interest totaling 2,026,917 shares, an increase of 24.5% from the March 31st total of 1,627,846 shares. Based on an average trading volume of 348,526 shares, the days-to-cover ratio is currently 5.8 days. Approximately 7.6% of the company's stock are sold short.

Wall Street Analysts Forecast Growth

A number of research firms recently issued reports on GWH. Wall Street Zen downgraded ESS Tech from a "hold" rating to a "sell" rating in a research note on Friday, January 23rd. Roth Mkm dropped their price target on ESS Tech from $3.50 to $2.50 and set a "buy" rating on the stock in a research note on Friday, March 6th. One investment analyst has rated the stock with a Buy rating, one has issued a Hold rating and one has issued a Sell rating to the company. According to data from MarketBeat.com, ESS Tech currently has a consensus rating of "Hold" and a consensus price target of $2.25.

Get Our Latest Stock Analysis on ESS Tech

ESS Tech Stock Performance

NYSE:GWH traded up $0.01 during mid-day trading on Friday, reaching $1.11. 436,937 shares of the stock were exchanged, compared to its average volume of 432,406. The company has a 50 day simple moving average of $1.25 and a 200-day simple moving average of $2.20. The stock has a market capitalization of $30.02 million, a P/E ratio of -0.26 and a beta of 1.43. The company has a quick ratio of 1.03, a current ratio of 1.04 and a debt-to-equity ratio of 1.08. ESS Tech has a 1 year low of $0.76 and a 1 year high of $13.87.

ESS Tech ( NYSE:GWH - Get Free Report) last released its quarterly earnings results on Thursday, March 5th. The company reported ($1.20) earnings per share (EPS) for the quarter, missing analysts' consensus estimates of ($0.76) by ($0.44). The firm had revenue of ($1.59) million for the quarter, compared to analysts' expectations of ($1.60) million. ESS Tech had a negative return on equity of 1,141.31% and a negative net margin of 1,045.28%. Analysts anticipate that ESS Tech will post -1 earnings per share for the current fiscal year.

Institutional Trading of ESS Tech

A number of hedge funds have recently added to or reduced their stakes in GWH. Citadel Advisors LLC acquired a new stake in ESS Tech during the 3rd quarter worth approximately $30,000. Yorkville Advisors Global LP acquired a new stake in ESS Tech during the 3rd quarter worth approximately $74,000. Green Alpha Advisors LLC lifted its position in ESS Tech by 1,016.7% during the 4th quarter. Green Alpha Advisors LLC now owns 118,683 shares of the company's stock worth $223,000 after acquiring an additional 108,055 shares during the period. Geode Capital Management LLC lifted its position in ESS Tech by 140.2% during the 4th quarter. Geode Capital Management LLC now owns 136,470 shares of the company's stock worth $257,000 after acquiring an additional 79,657 shares during the period. Finally, LPL Financial LLC acquired a new stake in ESS Tech during the 4th quarter worth approximately $27,000. 46.77% of the stock is owned by institutional investors and hedge funds.

About ESS Tech

( Get Free Report)

ESS Tech, Inc (NYSE: GWH) is a Portland, Oregon?based company specializing in long?duration iron flow battery energy storage solutions. The company's core business centers on the design, manufacture and deployment of modular battery systems that store electricity using an iron?chloride electrochemical process. These systems are engineered to support grid operators, utilities, commercial and industrial customers in integrating renewable power, managing peak loads and ensuring reliable back?up power.

At the heart of ESS Tech's offering is its Energy Warehouse, a containerized flow battery system featuring non?toxic, fully recyclable materials and a simple architecture that separates energy storage capacity from power output.

value.Title: Data centers must pay more for electricity, state regulators rule The order aims to shield residents and other businesses from the impact of data centers' enormous thirst for energy.

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHW-96B3-RSDH-71F0-00000-00Data centers must pay more for electricity, state regulators rule The order aims to shield residents and other businesses from the impact of data centers' enormous thirst for energy. 2026-05-08T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:09:37ZLexisNexisAPg. 007Data centers must pay more for electricity, state regulators rule The order aims to shield residents and other businesses from the impact of data centers' enormous thirst for energy. Gosia Wozniacka and Mike Rogoway - The Oregonian/OregonLive

The Oregon Public Utility Commission approved a major overhaul of how Portland General Electric charges data centers for power Thursday, mandating that the state's biggest electricity users bear more of the grid's long term costs.

The Commission's order creates a separate, legally distinct customer class for large data centers. It sets rules for how PGE allocates costs to the data centers and establishes new rates. And it includes enforcement mechanisms such as penalties and surcharges to ensure data centers pay for the costs they create.

The ruling does not specify how much data center rates will increase or what the impact on residential bills will be, but it requires PGE to update and implement new rates in June.

The decision marks the first implementation of the Power Act, a landmark Oregon law passed last year that aims to prevent large data centers from shifting the cost of the power plant and transmission upgrades required to serve them onto other customers. The law applies to Oregon's investor-owned utilities, chiefly PGE and PacifiCorp. The latter is required to develop its own rate design for data centers, though Thursday's order may set some precedent for how regulators implement the law for both utilities.

The Oregon Citizens' Utility Board, a nonprofit watchdog organization that advocates for utility customers, said the decision delivers on the Power Act's goal by putting data centers on track to pay higher, more accurate rates for the grid costs they create.

"This decision is overall a win for Oregonians," said Charlotte Shuff, a spokesperson with the board. "We are confident ... we will see data centers paying higher rates that more accurately reflect the costs they are putting on our energy grid."

PGE declined to comment on the specifics of the proposal but indicated it shares the Commission's goals.

"Our focus remains on maintaining reliable service, supporting economic development and protecting residential and small business customers from unnecessary cost impacts," said John McFarland, the company's chief customer officer.

The Commission's order appears more forceful than what PGE originally proposed.

It requires data centers to keep paying for power plants and transmission lines built to serve their demand for as long as those facilities are needed -- even decades later -- rather than for a limited number of years, as PGE had proposed.

It also sets higher minimum payments than PGE had proposed to cover the costs of additional transmission lines and power generation to support data centers' enormous electricity demand.

The order includes provisions intended to encourage the deployment of renewable power by giving data centers and PGE more flexibility in how they procure power.

And it imposes a 1-cent per kilowatt-hour surcharge on very large data centers, with the revenue directed to energy efficiency, repairs and local energy generation like rooftop solar. It's unclear how many data centers would have to pay the surcharge. Residential customers already pay a similar surcharge for clean energy and energy efficiency programs.

Nearly all the data centers PGE serves are in Washington County. They include data hosting companies like Digital Realty, Flexential, NTT Inc. and QTS, which leases space to Facebook parent Meta. None of the companies responded to inquiries Thursday about how the ruling affects them.

PGE must set and file new rates to match the commission's order by June 3 -- and implement them by June 10.

All parties to the case have 60 days to ask the Commission to reconsider its decision, after which the order may be appealed to the Oregon Court of Appeals.

Unlike PGE's case, PacifiCorp's implementation remains an open, contested proceeding before the Commission.

The Citizens' Utility Board has said PacifiCorp has not clearly explained how it plans to implement the new law or protect customers from the costs of large data center growth. The group argues PacifiCorp has not been fully transparent about its rate design or how it plans to allocate data center-driven costs, making it difficult for regulators and the public to evaluate whether the utility is complying with the law's intent.

Regulators are expected to issue an initial ruling in PacifiCorp's case this summer, with a final decision at a later date. PacifiCorp must have special rates for data centers in place by 2028.

Data centers are almost entirely responsible for a huge surge in Oregon energy consumption over the past several years. Power planners anticipate energy demand will climb even faster in the years ahead, fueled by the unrelenting demand for electricity from artificial intelligence systems.

Oregon lawmakers passed the Power Act in 2025 aiming to prevent that surge in demand from driving up rates for everyone. Data centers unsuccessfully fought the bill, arguing it was unfair to single out one industry.

The Power Act applies to customers of the state's privately owned utilities but not to municipal systems or electric cooperatives, like those that power Amazon's huge constellation of data centers in Morrow and Umatilla counties. Utilities owned by the public or their members already had the authority to allocate data centers' costs directly to that industry.

Data centers, like this QTS facility in Hillsboro, are responsible for a huge increase in Oregon power use. Thursday's order aims to shield residents and other businesses from the costs of supplying the tech industry. Sean Meagher, file

May 5th, 2026 ( Ticker Report - Delivered by Newstex )

ESS Tech ( NYSE:GWH - Get Free Report) will likely be announcing its Q1 2026 results after the market closes on Thursday, May 7th. Analysts expect the company to announce earnings of ($0.29) per share and revenue of $0.40 million for the quarter. Interested persons may review the information on the company's upcoming Q1 2026 earning report for the latest details on the call scheduled for Thursday, May 7, 2026 at 5:00 PM ET.

ESS Tech ( NYSE:GWH - Get Free Report) last posted its quarterly earnings results on Thursday, March 5th. The company reported ($1.20) earnings per share (EPS) for the quarter, missing the consensus estimate of ($0.76) by ($0.44). ESS Tech had a negative net margin of 1,045.28% and a negative return on equity of 1,141.31%. The firm had revenue of ($1.59) million for the quarter, compared to analyst estimates of ($1.60) million. On average, analysts expect ESS Tech to post $-1 EPS for the current fiscal year and $-1 EPS for the next fiscal year.

ESS Tech Stock Performance

GWH traded up $0.01 during trading hours on Wednesday, hitting $1.16. 331,169 shares of the stock were exchanged, compared to its average volume of 520,270. The stock has a market cap of $31.39 million, a price-to-earnings ratio of -0.27 and a beta of 1.43. The stock has a 50-day simple moving average of $1.25 and a 200-day simple moving average of $2.08. The company has a debt-to-equity ratio of 1.08, a current ratio of 1.04 and a quick ratio of 1.03. ESS Tech has a 12 month low of $0.76 and a 12 month high of $13.87.

Institutional Trading of ESS Tech

A number of large investors have recently modified their holdings of GWH. Citadel Advisors LLC acquired a new position in ESS Tech during the 3rd quarter valued at about $30,000. Engineers Gate Manager LP acquired a new stake in shares of ESS Tech in the fourth quarter valued at approximately $31,000. State Street Corp increased its stake in shares of ESS Tech by 188.1% in the fourth quarter. State Street Corp now owns 46,975 shares of the company's stock valued at $88,000 after purchasing an additional 30,671 shares during the period. Geode Capital Management LLC raised its holdings in shares of ESS Tech by 140.2% during the fourth quarter. Geode Capital Management LLC now owns 136,470 shares of the company's stock valued at $257,000 after buying an additional 79,657 shares during the last quarter. Finally, Arosa Capital Management LP boosted its position in shares of ESS Tech by 40.7% during the fourth quarter. Arosa Capital Management LP now owns 950,000 shares of the company's stock worth $1,786,000 after buying an additional 274,583 shares during the period. Institutional investors and hedge funds own 46.77% of the company's stock.

Analyst Upgrades and Downgrades

A number of research analysts have recently issued reports on GWH shares. Weiss Ratings restated a "sell (e+)" rating on shares of ESS Tech in a research report on Monday. Roth Mkm dropped their price objective on shares of ESS Tech from $3.50 to $2.50 and set a "buy" rating on the stock in a research note on Friday, March 6th. Finally, Wall Street Zen cut shares of ESS Tech from a "hold" rating to a "sell" rating in a report on Friday, January 23rd. One equities research analyst has rated the stock with a Buy rating, one has assigned a Hold rating and one has given a Sell rating to the stock. According to MarketBeat, the company presently has an average rating of "Hold" and a consensus target price of $2.25.

View Our Latest Stock Analysis on ESS Tech

ESS Tech Company Profile

( Get Free Report)

ESS Tech, Inc (NYSE: GWH) is a Portland, Oregon?based company specializing in long?duration iron flow battery energy storage solutions. The company's core business centers on the design, manufacture and deployment of modular battery systems that store electricity using an iron?chloride electrochemical process. These systems are engineered to support grid operators, utilities, commercial and industrial customers in integrating renewable power, managing peak loads and ensuring reliable back?up power.

At the heart of ESS Tech's offering is its Energy Warehouse, a containerized flow battery system featuring non?toxic, fully recyclable materials and a simple architecture that separates energy storage capacity from power output.

value.Title: Why PacifiCorp is betting big on California 's energy market PacifiCorp is the first utility outside California to participate in the market, which pools generation and transmission assets to reduce costs and improve reliability.

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJ7-KN03-SG1V-M10D-00000-00Why PacifiCorp is betting big on California's energy market PacifiCorp is the first utility outside California to participate in the market, which pools generation and transmission assets to reduce costs and improve reliability. 2026-05-10T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T15:59:05ZLexisNexisCPg. 004Why PacifiCorp is betting big on California's energy market PacifiCorp is the first utility outside California to participate in the market, which pools generation and transmission assets to reduce costs and improve reliability. Ted Sickinger - The Oregonian/OregonLive

PacifiCorp this month became the first utility outside California to join a new wholesale energy market, a move it says will generate substantial savings for customers, greater reliability and reduced greenhouse gas emissions.

The Portland-based utility, Oregon's second largest, joined the California Independent System Operator's extended day-ahead market, or EDAM, which went live May 1. CAISO is a nonprofit that manages the high voltage grid for 80% of California and part of Nevada, and is now extending participation in its day-ahead market to utilities outside its borders.

In effect, PacifiCorp is pooling its generation and transmission assets with other utilities and allowing CAISO to determine how to optimize the flow of power to meet each utility's forecast demand with the lowest cost power and transmission.

PacifiCorp is the first utility outside California to go live in the market, which includes that state's biggest investor-owned utilities: Southern California Edison, Pacific Gas & Electric and San Diego Gas & Electric.

PacifiCorp has a small number of customers in California but has not previously participated in the day-ahead market. It's a key player in its expansion, however, because it operates in six states and owns the largest privately held transmission system in the western United States. Other utilities are expected to follow suit, which would increase the market's efficiency, according to Mike Wilding, vice president of energy supply management at PacifiCorp.

"These organized markets are the best tool that we have available to control our costs for our customers," Wilding said. "The way they do that is they leverage larger footprints, a larger pool of generation and more transmission connectivity than PacifiCorp has on its own, which now means you can move electricity where it needs to go more effectively and more efficiently."

Portland General Electric has pledged to join the market in October, and other utilities in California and New Mexico are slated to join in 2027. Utilities in Idaho and Nevada are also evaluating joining.

Regionwide coordination of energy markets has been on the table for decades but has been a bugaboo for policymakers and utilities in the Northwest, where the transmission system is highly fragmented into individual utility fiefdoms.

But the need for better coordination and resource sharing has become more acute due to surging industrial energy demand, forecast supply shortfalls that could undermine reliability, and rising prices.

PacifiCorp's modeling shows EDAM could eventually save its customers 00 million annually. In addition, Wilding said the market will free up transmission capacity and boost reliability by enabling regions with excess power to assist areas experiencing extreme weather. And it will support greenhouse gas reductions by managing variations in output of renewable energy, like wind and solar, across a wider area.

The day-ahead market builds on the success of an energy imbalance market that CAISO launched in 2014. PacificCorp was also the first to join that market, which gives utilities access to energy to manage short term supply and demand imbalances. It spans 11 western states and has generated over .6 billion in benefits for participants since it launched.

One big player that won't be joining EDAM is the Bonneville Power Administration, which sells power from 31 hydroelectric dams and a nuclear plant, and which operates three quarters of the high voltage grid in the Northwest. Under pressure from customers leery of giving California too much control over the region's power grid, BPA last year decided to join Markets+, a separate wholesale market operated by the Southwest Power Pool and headquartered in Arkansas.

At the time, BPA said it preferred the governance structure and reliability program of Markets+, despite studies showing more substantial savings from joining EDAM.

The federal agency's decision is being challenged in the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals by a coalition of environmental and consumer groups. They contend the decision will drive up energy bills in the region, undermine reliability and decrease access to renewables. The groups say the decision violates BPA's obligations under the Northwest Power Act to ensure cost effective and reliable power for the Northwest and protect fish and wildlife in the Columbia River Basin.

The California Independent System Operator has made significant changes to its own governance structure to accommodate expansion of the day-ahead market, transitioning to a new decision-making authority and laying the plans for a new regional organization.

PacifiCorp has joined an energy market run by the California Independent System Operator, or CAISO. Courtesy CAISO

value.Title: Experts: Data centers make power grid planning hard
value.Overview: Statesman Journal (Salem, Oregon), NEWS; Pg. A1, 1445words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHC-MT33-RTGT-N1JG-00000-00Experts: Data centers make power grid planning hard2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:09:37ZLexisNexisNEWSPg. A1Experts: Data centers make power grid planning hardByMonica SamayoaOregon Public Broadcasting

The proliferation of power-hungry data centers is making it difficult to predict the future of the Pacific Northwest's energy needs.

That has implications for the region's efforts to invest in renewable energy sources, and may affect the cost of electricity for consumers in the years ahead.

So far, Oregon has more than 120 data centers across the state, and more are in development.

According to an analysis published in April by Energy + Environmental Economics, the region is expected to build data centers that consume about 3 gigawatts of electricity by 2030 - enough energy to power 2.25 million homes.

But other experts predict data centers could demand far less electricity than that.

A single data center might require as much energy as it takes to power a small city like Ashland, where about 21,000 people live.

Another might need as much energy as it takes to power Eugene, which has nearly 10 times as many residents.

It depends on the size of the data center and whether it's powering artificial intelligence or hosting servers for cloud storage.

With multiple technology companies in different stages of developing data centers across the Northwest, it's impossible to get a precise estimate of just how much energy these high-tech projects will collectively require.

Elaine Hart, co-founder and principal at Sylvan Energy Analytics based in Portland, said that's making it hard to develop plans for an electrical grid that meets the region's needs.

"We're kind of at a point of unprecedented uncertainty, which is very scary for planners, and it means we need solutions that are kind of robust to a lot of different possibilities," she said.

Growing energy gap

In April, Energy + Environmental Economics, known as E3, released the second phase of a study that looks into the greater Pacific Northwest's long-term energy needs. The study was commissioned by 22 electric utilities across the region to look at energy resources in Oregon, Washington, Idaho, Montana, and portions of Utah and Wyoming.

It found that much of the region's need to bring new electricity sources online is unrelated to data centers - population growth and economic development are pushing up energy demand, and the region is not moving fast enough to address energy gaps left by retired coal power plants.

Add a modest amount of data center expansion in the Pacific Northwest to the equation, and the six-state region is facing a 9-gigawatt energy gap by 2030.

That's the average amount of energy consumed by the entire state of Oregon, and if power companies don't find new sources of electricity to meet that growing need, the region's grid could struggle to deliver enough energy to power electric heaters during extreme cold or cooling systems during heat waves.

Looking further ahead, the E3 study says the gap between how much energy the region needs and how much is available could double, to as high as 18 gigawatts by 2035.

"We find that, in the near term, we really aren't on track to fill that gap, and there's a wide variety of market and institutional and other barriers that have really gotten in the way of the industry and the region," E3's senior partner Arne Olsen said during a webinar in April.

And these estimates could be much higher, "if we assumed a more aggressive data center build out in our study," Olsen said.

Unclogging an energy bottleneck

Oregon and Washington face major challenges that could affect how well these states are able to meet the energy needs of the future.

An OPB and ProPublica investigation found that the Bonneville Power Administration, which owns 75% of the region's power grid, has a sluggish process for adding new transmission lines necessary for developers of new electric power sources.

That's slowed efforts to develop large-scale wind and solar energy projects to meet the region's needs. Oregon's and Washington's renewable power development ranks near the bottom in the nation due to the lack of available transmission lines.

E3's report called that transmission bottleneck an obstacle to addressing future energy needs west of the Oregon and Washington Cascades, where energy demand is already strained and expected to grow significantly more.

Building transmission will be key to unclogging that bottleneck, it found.

Both states have laws that require their electric utilities to deliver carbon-free energy by mid-century, but the slow-moving pace of permitting and siting of these developments is contributing to regional power gaps.

The Trump administration has also rescinded billions of dollars originally allocated toward climate action that would have helped both states accelerate the addition of renewable energy sources.

And while both states are moving away from fossil fuel-generated electricity, E3's analysis found the region may need to hold on to natural gas for a little longer.

They found that gas-fired plants could help with grid reliability during peak energy moments.

Natural gas power plants generate more than a third of the electricity consumed in Oregon, and about a fifth of the electricity consumed in Washington.

But the study's authors cautioned against overbuilding new natural gas plants, which could push up already-high electric bills.

Hart, with Sylvan Energy Analytics, agreed that natural gas has an important role to play in the near term. But she said it should be a last resort when considering new sources of electricity.

"If we've got investments in natural gas happening because of data center demand, that's a tough one," she said. "We need to be clear-eyed about and thoughtful about who's paying for it, and what's being done to reduce the utilization of those plants. The best thing we can do to reduce utilization of natural gas plants in the region is to build renewable energy."

Data center demand response

Sylvan Energy Analytics did an independent study looking into E3's initial findings.

That study also forecast a major gap between what the six-state region will need and how much energy is already being generated. It found the gap could be smaller than the 9 gigawatt energy gap E3 predicts, however.

The independent study predicted a 6 gigawatt gap - but said the gap could be as low as 1 gigawatt.

That huge range in forecasts, driven by uncertainty around how many data centers may be built and how much energy they will use, makes it hard for grid planners to determine how many more renewable energy developments the region may need to build out.

And if the region overbuilds these developments, those costs could show up on utility bills - hurting ratepayers, Hart said.

As power companies and policymakers look to generate more electricity and build powerlines to meet the energy demand of the future, the Sylvan Energy Analytics report maps out another way to shrink the gap between how much power is available and how much the region needs.

It sees energy efficiency, driven by developing technology that uses less electricity to perform the same task, as a key tool.

Another high-tech solution is known as "demand response," which means using less power during peak moments. Some technology already uses demand response, like smart thermostats that adjust the amount of energy used.

Hart said the same concept should be taken up by data centers during the cold winter or hot summer days, when energy use is highest.

Tech companies with data centers around the globe could shift computing tasks to other locations when the grid is strained. That could significantly help the region save energy.

"If you have the ability to call the data centers first, in that sort of emergency circumstance, and curtail their output before affecting residential and small commercial customers, under some of the scenarios we might be OK," she said. "We might only need to build a little bit more than what's already sort of coming down the pike."

This type of power flexibility is already widely being used in Texas, she said. But that required state legislation. Google says it has tried a demand response pilot in The Dalles.

Hart said she doesn't expect enough data centers to power down voluntarily to address Oregon's needs.

"I don't think they'll do it on their own," she said. "It might require legislation, and other states are taking up this question as well."

But during Oregon's short 2026 legislative session, bills focused on helping the grid, with solutions like balcony solar and virtual power plants, did not make it to Gov. Tina Kotek's desk for signature.

The virtual power plant bill would have helped relieve some strain on the grid by harnessing and distributing energy generated in homes during high peak moments.

It's unclear if Oregon lawmakers plan on bringing up the bills again next year.
